reasoning: This is the well-known official 1Password AUR package that downloads a PGP-signed, checksum-verified binary tarball from 1Password's official CDN. The two 'unsafe' findings from qwen are both false positives. (1) aur.env contains op:// references which are maintainer CI infrastructure placeholders (used only in the maintainer's GitLab CI to publish the package), NOT something executed during makepkg build/package/prepare or in .install scripts. It poses no risk to a user installing the package. (2) The eval-with-heredoc templating in install_biometrics_policy.sh and after-install.sh operates on a template file shipped inside the signed upstream tarball, substituting POLICY_OWNERS derived from /etc/passwd. This is a code-quality concern at most (theoretical injection requires an attacker who can already create users with shell-metacharacter names as root), not a supply-chain attack. Critically, these scripts (install.sh/after-install.sh/install_biometrics_policy.sh) are NOT invoked by the PKGBUILD package() function or the .install hooks — they are 1Password's own bundled deb/rpm installer scripts that ship inside the tarball and are not executed by makepkg. deepseek correctly assessed all files as safe.
disagreement_analysis: The disagreement stems from qwen flagging two files. aur.env was reviewed only by qwen (deepseek did not select it) and qwen overreacted to op:// secret-reference URIs that are maintainer CI config, not build-time executed code. For install_biometrics_policy.sh, both models saw the eval pattern but deepseek correctly classified it as a code-quality issue (input from root-controlled /etc/passwd, no network/exfiltration) while qwen escalated it to UNSAFE on a theoretical privilege-escalation/template-tampering scenario that ignores that the template is part of the PGP-signed upstream tarball.
coverage_issues: deepseek did not review aur.env (qwen did); however aur.env is not build-relevant so this is a minor gap.
Neither model could inspect the contents of the signed binary tarball itself, but it is PGP-verified against a known validpgpkey, which is the appropriate trust anchor for a vendor binary package.
false_positives: aur.env flagged as UNSAFE (qwen): op:// references are 1Password CLI secret placeholders for the maintainer's CI publishing pipeline; not executed during makepkg and not a user-facing risk.

install_biometrics_policy.sh flagged as UNSAFE (qwen): eval-on-template is a code-quality concern only; template is part of the signed upstream tarball and POLICY_OWNERS derives from root-controlled /etc/passwd. Also not invoked by PKGBUILD/.install during makepkg.
learnings: Models should distinguish between scripts bundled inside an upstream vendor tarball (1Password's own deb/rpm installer scripts) versus scripts actually invoked by PKGBUILD functions or .install hooks. The bundled installer scripts are NOT run by makepkg and should be assessed accordingly.

CI/maintainer infrastructure files like aur.env (op:// secret references, .gitlab-ci.yml) are not build-time executed and should generally be treated as out-of-scope unless referenced by build/package/prepare/check or .install — qwen over-escalated aur.env.

The eval-with-heredoc-on-shipped-template pattern is a recurring false-positive trigger; guidance should clarify that injection risk requires attacker-controlled input AND that the template here is PGP-verified upstream content, making it a low-severity code-quality note rather than an UNSAFE supply-chain finding.
When a PGP signature with a pinned validpgpkey verifies a vendor binary, models should treat that as the trust anchor rather than escalating on unreviewable binary contents.
File-selection divergence (only qwen picked aur.env) shows value in a consistent prioritization heuristic, but verdict logic should weight whether a flagged file is actually reachable during install.
